notes.anchor: that ruler of the commonwealth to whom we wished all things to be referred
notes.note: Cicero invokes the figure of the ideal statesman (the rector or moderator rei publicae) that he had developed in his dialogue De Re Publica; he is measuring the real Pompey against this philosophical ideal and finding him wanting.

notes.anchor: in the fifth book, I think, Scipio speaks
notes.note: Cicero quotes from the fifth book of his own De Re Publica (54--51 BC), where the principal speaker is Scipio Aemilianus; the passage likens the statesman's aim (the citizens' prosperity) to the helmsman's safe course and the general's victory.

notes.anchor: to stir up barbarian kings, to bring savage tribes into Italy under arms
notes.note: Cicero ascribes to Pompey a strategy of abandoning Italy in order to return at the head of foreign forces -- client kings of the East and barbarian levies; he condemns it as making the war a contest for personal mastery rather than a defence of the state.

The dictator L. Cornelius Sulla had seized Rome by force in 82 BC and ruled through proscription; Cicero charges that many in both camps now covet a Sulla-style autocracy, so that neither leader's aim is the citizens' good.

notes.anchor: not divining like that woman whom no one believed
notes.note: The unnamed woman is Cassandra, the Trojan prophetess fated by Apollo to foresee disaster yet never to be believed; Cicero distances his own foreboding from her ecstatic prophecy, claiming instead to forecast by reasoned conjecture.

notes.anchor: So great an Iliad of evils
notes.note: "An Iliad of evils" (Ilias kakon) was a Greek proverb for a vast accumulation of disasters, taking Homer's epic of the Trojan War as the byword for prolonged catastrophe.

notes.anchor: there is so much talk at Luceria
notes.note: Luceria in Apulia had been Pompey's headquarters and mustering-ground; the dilectus (conscription) reportedly proclaimed there alarmed Italy, though Cicero says the universal ruin to come is more to be feared than the levy itself.

notes.anchor: Would it were something like the Parthian affair
notes.note: Cicero wishes Caesar's rapid advance might end as disastrously for him as Crassus's invasion of Parthia did in 53 BC, when Crassus and his army were destroyed at Carrhae.

notes.anchor: a book by Demetrius of Magnesia
notes.note: Demetrius of Magnesia was a Greek scholar and friend of Atticus; the treatise Cicero wants, "On Concord" (Peri homonoias), suited his anxious meditation on whether concord between Caesar and Pompey was still possible.
